one_line_takeaway: A leakage-resistant, fully-automated Linux-kernel benchmark shows that open-source LLMs can flag vulnerable C code but reliably fail to tell it apart from its patched version and tend to generate oversimplified, unusable fixes.
key_finding: LLMs are markedly better at flagging vulnerable code than at recognizing that a patched block is safe, i.e. they over-predict 'vulnerable': aggregated accuracy was 66.51% on SVD3 (vulnerable blocks) vs only 33.19% on SVD4 (patched blocks), and 57.92% on SVD5 vs 40.13% on SVD6. Supplying the ground-truth CVE/CWE (SVD5/6) raised accuracy by about 9% on average. CVE ranking was near-useless (best Top-5 accuracy only 8.14% for Llama3.1-70b; several models at 0.00%), while CWE ranking was better (best Top-5 22.48% for Llama3-70b). For patching, models consistently produced patches with lower cyclomatic complexity and shorter length than the reference patches, often inserting placeholders like '...' or 'the rest of the code remains unchanged', indicating oversimplified, not directly usable fixes. Detection accuracy improved from L1 to L2 abstraction but degraded at L3; code length showed no consistent correlation with accuracy.
core_contribution: VulnLLMEval, a fully-automated benchmark framework and dataset for evaluating pre-trained LLMs on two software-security tasks: Software Vulnerability Detection (SVD) and Software Vulnerability Patching (SVP) on C code. The paper's own contributions are (1) an automated data-collection method that mines Linux-kernel commit hashes/CVE/CWE metadata to extract paired vulnerable and patched code blocks (functions plus non-functional elements like macros, globals, type defs) at file/function abstraction levels, eliminating the need for security experts to hand-label ground truth; (2) a curated dataset of 307 real-world vulnerabilities (285 unique CVEs, 30 CWEs) from the Linux kernel; (3) an empirical evaluation of ten open-source instruct LLMs across 8 prompt templates; and (4) an analysis of how CVE/CWE context, abstraction level, and code length affect detection accuracy. Note: prior datasets discussed in Related Work (CVEfixes, Devign, CodeXGLUE, REVEAL) and prior benchmark studies (Ullah et al., Khare et al., Sun et al., Liu et al., VulBench) are cited comparisons, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: This is a defensive-capability evaluation paper, not an attack paper; it does not posit an adversary or a formal threat model. The security concern motivating the work is the surge of real software vulnerabilities (over 29,000 CVEs addressed in 2023) and whether LLMs can automate detecting and patching them. A methodological security issue the paper explicitly guards against is data leakage / benchmark contamination: because LLMs are trained on public code including the Linux kernel without distinguishing vulnerable from patched versions, evaluating them on leaked datasets inflates results. The framework mitigates this by building a custom paired vulnerable/patched dataset and by withholding commit descriptions in SVD prompts to avoid revealing the answer.
limitations: Scope is limited to C code from the Linux kernel, so generalizability to other languages/projects is an open question (acknowledged under External Validity). LLM non-determinism persisted despite temperature 0. The CodeBLEU data-flow component broke down for blocks lacking functions (mostly non-functional elements), particularly hurting CWE-416 evaluation. Some models returned empty responses on SVP tasks, and preprocessing/filtering of blank outputs left different data subsets per model, complicating length comparisons. ChatGPT/GPT models were not evaluated. The GitHub repo was stated to be released only after acceptance. Automated regex-based extraction and scoring could contain bugs (mitigated by peer review of the pipeline).

datasets_used: Custom VulnLLMEval dataset: 307 real-world vulnerabilities from the Linux kernel (C code), 285 unique CVEs, 30 CWEs; paired vulnerable and patched code blocks
Vulnerable code blocks average 202 lines (range 3 to 3,581); patched blocks average 209 lines (range 3 to 3,613)
Metadata sourced from Linux kernel commit hashes, MITRE, and the NVD (using resources from Pereira et al.)
Top-5 CWE instance counts: CWE-119 Buffer Overflow (50), CWE-416 Use After Free (37), CWE-200 Information Exposure (31), CWE-476 NULL Pointer Dereference (27), CWE-20 Improper Input Validation (26)
quantitative_results: Aggregated detection accuracy: SVD3 (vulnerable) 66.51%, SVD4 (patched) 33.19%, SVD5 (vulnerable+CVE/CWE) 57.92%, SVD6 (patched+CVE/CWE) 40.13%
Supplying ground-truth CVE/CWE improved accuracy by ~9% on average (SVD5/6 vs SVD3/4)
CVE Top-5 accuracy: best Llama3.1-70b 8.14%, Llama3-70b 5.21%; Codellama-7b/34b, Mistral-7b, Mixtral, Gemma2-9b all 0.00%
CWE Top-5 accuracy: best Llama3-70b 22.48%, Llama3-8b 21.17%, Gemma2-27b 17.92%; Codellama-34b lowest at 6.33%
MRR: best CWE Llama3-70b 12.44, best CVE Llama3.1-70b 5.79
Abstraction levels (Llama3.1-8b, best model): L1 recall 75.18% / F1 59.72; L2 recall 86.79% / F1 63.01; L3 recall 84.07% / F1 62.50
Cyclomatic complexity: reference vulnerable/patched code ~13.8 for Codellama-7b block set; LLM-generated patches consistently lower (e.g. 0.7 to ~7.6 range), confirming oversimplification
Average generated-patch length consistently shorter than reference patches (e.g. Gemma2-27b patched-LLM 11 lines vs 76 reference)
baselines_compared: Ten open-source instruct LLMs compared against each other: CodeLlama-7B, CodeLlama-34B, Llama3-8B, Llama3-70B, Llama3.1-8B, Llama3.1-70B, Mistral-7B, Mixtral-8x7B, Gemma2-9B, Gemma2-27B (all instruct, temperature 0.0)
Zero-shot (SVP1) vs few-shot with commit-description context (SVP2) compared for patching

Framework-level comparison (Table 1) against five prior benchmark studies: Ullah et al. [52], Khare et al. [22], Sun et al. [48], Liu et al. [28], and VulBench/Gao et al. [13] on criteria like real-world data, novel data collection, automated evaluation, no security experts for labeling
No traditional (non-LLM) detector baseline such as a static analyzer, fuzzer, or APR tool is empirically run; those are discussed only qualitatively as motivation
